metadata.title: Canadian politicians' rhetoric on Twitter/X: Analysing prejudice and inclusion towards Muslims using structural topic modelling and rhetorical analysis

abstract: We analysed tweets from five English-speaking Canadian political parties in the year leading up to the 2019 federal election to explore both prejudicial and inclusive rhetoric in relation to Muslim identities on social media. We used structural topic modelling to understand what topics were discussed before moving to a rhetorical approach to analyse how topics were discussed. We identified 10 topics. Seven talked about Muslim groups in primarily inclusive ways, including depicting the positive contributions to Canadian society, creating ideological space for Muslim religious practices and invoking superordinate identities with victims of hate crimes to cultivate solidarity. However, the effectiveness of inclusive rhetoric was sometimes questioned due to omitting the subgroup-specific prejudice faced by Muslims. Prejudicial rhetoric occurred in three of the topics due to the nativist populist PPC party depicting Muslims as a threat to Canadian values, as hostile to people from other religious faiths, and depicting 'elites' in society as concealing the 'true' information concerning Muslims. The study contributes to understanding how politicians attempt to cultivate minority inclusion/exclusion in multicultural contexts through social media, as well as understanding the rhetoric of nativist populism in Canada and its similarities to other Global North contexts.

paragraphs: Canadian society is characterized by religious diversity due, partly, to continuing trends of openness to immigration (Cornelissen, 2021). This diversity presents challenges for intergroup respect and cooperation. Muslims constitute a small percentage of the Canadian population (3.7%) but are one of the fastest growing religious minority groups in the country (Morency et al., 2017). Like other Global North nations in the post-9/11 political climate, Canadian Muslims have experienced increasing Islamophobia (Wilkins-Laflamme, 2018), which has severe consequences. Examples include the family of four murdered in a vehicle attack in London Ontario (BBC, 2021) and the Quebec Mosque shootings where six were killed and 19 injured (CBC, 2017). However, Islamophobia is not confined to the violent extremes. Canadian census data show that Muslims are the least liked of any religious minority in the country (Wilkins-Laflamme, 2018) and these prejudicial attitudes filter into everyday acts of discrimination, including harassment on public transport (Rieti, 2015) and barriers to work (Hanniman, 2008).

Politicians are a crucial source for influence on intergroup attitudes given their continuous efforts to shape public discourse (Reicher & Hopkins, 2001) and their access to large-scale communication platforms amplifying their reach (Haste, 2004). Previous analyses of politicians' Muslim-specific rhetoric have focused on European or US contexts, with little work in the Canadian context (Wilkins-Laflamme, 2018). Moreover, research has concentrated mainly on prejudicial forms, neglecting rhetoric aimed at cultivating inclusion. Last, politicians employ various dissemination tools, and social media is emerging as a significant addition. Presently, we conduct an analysis of Canadian politicians' prejudicial and inclusive rhetoric about Muslims on Twitter. Although this platform has been renamed (now X), we collected and analysed our data when under its original name and will therefore use this going forward. The research contributes towards understanding the rhetorical forms directed at Muslims within a relatively under-researched Global North context. Additionally, it highlights the importance of political rhetoric on social media by examining how politicians attempt to shape public narratives through these platforms.

sections.header_raw: The normalization of prejudice: influencing social norms through political rhetoric
sections.header_norm: the normalization of prejudice influencing social norms through political rhetoric
paragraphs: Anti-Muslim attitudes in Global North nations are commonly characterized by negative evaluations, and emotions such as fear, anger and disgust, directed towards Muslims (Lee et al., 2009). The development of prejudice is multi-faceted, but as national leaders, politicians have substantial influence over socially shared narratives, implicating them in intergroup attitudes broadly (Reicher & Hopkins, 2001). The significance of understanding politicians' prejudicial rhetoric can be seen in its impacts on the attitudes of the constructed ingroup (who 'we' are) towards the constructed outgroup (who 'they' are). For example, a Gallup poll revealed that Americans' attitudes towards immigration were more negative after (vs. before) Trump's campaign announcement speech (Flores, 2018). Similarly, Ruisch and Ferguson (2022) showed that support for President Trump predicted increased prejudice towards ethnic and religious minorities, including Muslims, over 1-5 year periods. Additionally, exposure to antiimmigrant rhetoric used by nativist populist politicians in Europe relates to higher anti-immigration attitudes (Schmuck & Matthes, 2019;Sheets et al., 2016).

An important factor contributing to increased prejudice is social norm shifts, where exposure to prejudicial rhetoric normalizes stronger expressions of prejudice (Ruisch & Ferguson, 2022). This normalization poses a significant challenge, particularly in how people interpret their experiences of discrimination. Islamophobia can negatively affect Muslims' sense of national belonging if they perceive it as a reflection of broader attitudes rather than localized actions (Kunst et al., 2012). This captures one of the more toxic aspects of politicians' Islamophobic rhetoric: Due to their status as leaders, there is greater potential that the people being targeted perceive it as representative of prevailing societal attitudes.
To understand the cultivation of intergroup hate, researchers adopting rhetorical perspectives have investigated how social actors construct minority identities as antagonistic outgroups (Reicher

| 859 PREJUDICE AND INCLUSION ON TWITTER/X et al., 2005). Common anti-Muslim narratives found in Global North contexts include invoking 'security threat' through associations with terrorist ideology to depict the physical safety of the national ingroup as threatened by Muslims (Hogan & Haltinner, 2015). Furthermore, 'cultural threat' is used to depict Muslims as wanting to change representations of the national identity towards their own forms. This involves rhetorical claims that 'our' rituals, spaces and behaviours are being replaced by 'theirs' (Portice & Reicher, 2018;Wood & Finlay, 2008).

Social actors also put forth arguments that cultivate prejudice in ways that align with anti-prejudice norms (Dixon & Levine, 2012). Ethno-religious discrimination can be legitimated by attacking abstract belief systems (i.e., Islamic religion), rather than Muslims as a group, thereby warding off accusations of prejudice (Verkuyten, 2013). Additionally, anti-Muslim rhetoric can be combined with anti-elite rhetoric so that acceptance of religious minorities is portrayed as serving 'elites' (commonly liberal/leftist) against the supposed wishes of 'the public' (Wood & Finlay, 2008). Such constructions serve to position speakers' anti-Muslim claims as 'truly' representing public interests, while also obscuring their attacks on religious minorities by targeting those in positions of power (Sakki & Pettersson, 2016). Thus, an important part of challenging prejudice is in understanding the narratives used to justify the exclusion of religious minority identities.

sections.header_raw: Beyond prejudicial rhetoric: examining inclusion and entrepreneurs of solidarity
sections.header_norm: beyond prejudicial rhetoric examining inclusion and entrepreneurs of solidarity
paragraphs: Although understanding prejudicial rhetoric contributes towards challenging negative attitudes, the relevance of 'entrepreneurs of solidarity' has been relatively neglected (Reicher et al., 2005). This refers to constructing ingroup boundaries in ways that include minority groups. Increasing the perceptual boundaries of ingroup membership encourages people to offer help to individuals viewed as members of the expanded ingroup (Levine et al., 2005;Levine & Thompson, 2004). It is therefore equally important to examine how social actors attempt to increase these perceptual boundaries, particularly when it comes to mobilizing support for marginalized groups.

For example, an analysis of texts from 1938 relating to Bulgarian national debates over Nazi Germany's order to deport Jewish Bulgarian citizens suggested inclusive rhetoric supported efforts to resist the order and save lives (Reicher et al., 2006). Various social actors sought to expand the categorical boundaries of ingroup membership to include Jewish people. Prominent voices like law societies and politicians depicted the forced deportation as a threat to the moral norms of the Bulgarian identity and its international reputation as a civil and free society. In contemporary Global North nations, the status of refugees and asylum seekers remains a perennial topic frequently discussed in ways that exclude these individuals from belonging (Chouliaraki & Zaborowski, 2017). Conversely, a more inclusive approach can be employed. In UK parliamentary debates, the category of 'human beings' has been used to foster a shared identity by positioning asylum seekers within a moral ingroup and depicting an ethical responsibility to aid those within it (Kirkwood, 2017).

Inclusion can also be cultivated by constructing superordinate identities. The common ingroup identity model proposes that if people can be induced to perceive outgroups as included in a superordinate identity, pro-ingroup biases become prominent and prejudice is reduced (Gaertner et al., 1993; for review see Dovidio et al., 2009). There are limits, however, on how willing people are to adopt common identities. Among high ingroup identifiers, attempts to induce superordinate categorizations can produce backlash and increase outgroup prejudice, as members try to reassert their original ingroup identity (Turner & Crisp, 2010). Furthermore, if there are disagreements over what the superordinate identity means, like how Democrats and Republicans understand being 'American', then creating inclusion through a superordinate identity can fail (Rutchick & Eccleston, 2010).

An alternative option is to cultivate dual identities, in which subgroups are maintained within a superordinate category (Dovidio et al., 2009). However, it is crucial for such rhetorical work to acknowledge subgroup specifics, like the particular forms of social injustice a group faces (McNeill & Vollhardt, 2020). Consider two contrasting examples from a US context. The Occupy movement constructed a superordinate identity of 'The 99%' to mobilize support. This identity sought to depict a shared collective interest in emancipation from a financial elite, but the injustices of different subgroups were acknowledged and legitimized within it (Rehmann, 2013). Conversely, the superordinate identification 'All Lives Matter', used as a rhetorical counterpoint to Black Lives Matter, sought to undermine recognition of the specific, systemic injustices endured by Black individuals in the United States and delegitimize attempts to mobilize against injustice (McNeill & Vollhardt, 2020). Thus, not all forms of superordinate categorization contribute positively to intergroup dynamics.

There can also be implicit limits on what aspects of subgroup identity are included in superordinate categories that favour the status quo for majority groups (Dovidio et al., 2009). In relation to religious minorities, this can involve championing national identification and access to mainstream platforms for Muslims in Europe who criticize Islam and adopt secular values, while excluding those who adhere to Islamic religious practices (Bangstad, 2013). Similarly, European education systems can restrict conceptions of inclusion to respect for diverse languages and diets, while marginalizing Islamic values and practices (Rissanen, 2021). Hence, inclusive rhetoric can be a conduit to cultivating expanded ingroup boundaries and challenging prejudice (Rehmann, 2013;Reicher et al., 2006). Yet it can also exclude minority identities that do not align with ingroup social norms and, therefore, only acknowledge inequality and prejudice along the prescribed lines of the dominant group (Bangstad, 2013;Rissanen, 2021). These two goals of enabling entrepreneurs of solidarity to be more effective, while also ensuring inclusive rhetoric is not taken for granted as eliciting solidarity, make it incumbent upon social psychologists to extend their analytic focus to inclusive rhetoric.

sections.header_raw: The current study: politicians' prejudicial and inclusive rhetoric on twitter
sections.header_norm: the current study politicians prejudicial and inclusive rhetoric on twitter
paragraphs: The value of analysing rhetoric from a process-oriented perspective is in understanding the social identity-based mechanisms through which political leaders attempt to shape public understandings (Reicher & Hopkins, 2001). Research on rhetorical identity construction often focuses on traditional forms of political communication (e.g., speeches, news articles, party manifestos; Dixon & Levine, 2012;Verkuyten, 2013;Wood & Finlay, 2008). Although more recent research has turned its attention to social media (Pettersson et al., 2023;Sakki & Pettersson, 2016), such analyses remain limited.

The relationship between a politician's Twitter statements and the public's awareness of them is not straightforward. Social media users who follow politicians are not representative of the general population (Minot et al., 2021). Additionally, such platforms foster self-selection, where users seek information consistent with their existing attitudes (Heiss & Matthes, 2020). Nevertheless, Twitter can have a significant impact during critical political moments, like national elections, when tweets reach beyond followers (Gross & Johnson, 2016). Furthermore, there can be a reciprocal relationship between broadcast and social media as journalists report politicians' tweets, thereby amplifying their reach and impacting wider public discourses (Ross & Caldwell, 2020).

Analysing politicians' rhetorical constructions about intergroup relationships on Twitter is crucial for several reasons: It has become an essential communication platform for political parties (Gross & Johnson, 2016); it provides the ability to reach diverse audiences without gatekeepers (Ross & Caldwell, 2020); and the unrestricted access enables discussion of any topic, including those considered inappropriate or unnewsworthy by traditional media producers. Presently, we analyse tweets to provide insights into the ways intergroup issues are constructed by politicians. Research on political rhetoric in a given context often focuses on a select number of parties/politicians or prejudicial narratives (Heiss & Matthes, 2020;Minot et al., 2021;Sakki & Pettersson, 2016). We seek a more representative understanding of the political rhetoric used by Canadian politicians about Muslims/Islam. Leveraging Twitter enables us to include politicians from across the political spectrum, explore beyond party leaders and examine prejudicial and inclusive rhetoric.

Research on rhetorical identity construction often focuses on traditional forms of political communication (e.g., speeches, news articles, party manifestos; Dixon & Levine, 2012;Verkuyten, 2013;Wood & Finlay, 2008). sections.paper_id: Shayegh et al. - 2024 - Canadian politicians' rhetoric on TwitterX Analysing prejudice and inclusion towards Muslims using
paragraphs: We sought to identify the general topics addressed by politicians when discussing Muslims and examine the content of those topics in relation to intergroup processes. We used a combination of Structural Topic Modelling (STM) (Roberts et al., 2014) and a rhetorical social-psychological approach (Billig et al., 1988). STM is a quantitative topic modelling procedure that groups text segments based on probabilities of word co-occurrences (Roberts et al., 2019). A rhetorical social psychological approach focuses on how language can be used to depict versions of social reality that reflect implicit ideologies and serve socio-political goals (Billig et al., 1988;Condor et al., 2013). The analysis was guided by three research questions:
RQ1: What topics do Canadian politicians discuss in relation to Muslim identities? RQ2: How do Canadian politicians discuss the topics related to Muslim identities?
RQ3: What are the potential political goals informing how these topics are discussed?

The combination of topic modelling and qualitative analyses has proven effective in studying different social and political contexts (e.g., Benites-Lazaro et al., 2018;Brookes & McEnery, 2019;Törnberg & Törnberg, 2016). The qualitative component benefits from more representative data than is possible with manual inquiry (Stubbs, 1997), whereas the quantitative component benefits from a richer analysis of language beyond descriptive overviews (Baker et al., 2008).

However, we did not view this combination as epistemological triangulation, where each given finding is validated through separate methods, because we judged our research questions contained a range of criteria for assessing validity. When analysing text data one can distinguish what is being said, how it is being said and why it is being said (Baker et al., 2008;Sakki & Pettersson, 2016). The 'what' category is most compatible with computational methods because representing topic categories can benefit from using word co-occurrence probabilities (Roberts et al., 2014). Assessing how something was said, and the potential functions of why it was said that way, are less compatible because probability has little relevance for interpretive meaning (Baker et al., 2008). In rhetorical analyses, one needs to consider the variety of ideological norms within a given social context and how these potentially informed why a rhetorical construction was used rather than other available alternatives (Billig et al., 1988). Although we believe each approach can benefit from the other, we consider this along the lines of what others have termed 'methodological pluralism' (Törnberg & Uitermark, 2021); in that, each method provides a valuable, but different, perspective on the same social phenomena. Consequently, we structured our analysis in two phases, each operating under its own epistemological guidelines.

sections.paper_id: Shayegh et al. - 2024 - Canadian politicians' rhetoric on TwitterX Analysing prejudice and inclusion towards Muslims using
paragraphs: To ensure a representative sample of Canadian politicians' rhetoric on Twitter, we collected data from the timelines of as many politicians as possible from the five main English-speaking political parties: Liberal Party, Conservative Party, New Democratic Party (NDP), Green Party and the People's Party of Canada (PPC). We focused on English statements due to researcher language constraints and issues with alteration of meaning from externally translated materials (Liddicoat, 2009). This meant the exclusion of Bloc Québécois.

We focused on the rhetoric in the lead-up to the 2019 Canadian federal election and consulted the list of all political party candidates on the Election Canada website. Initially, we found 915 handles and collected tweets between 21 October 2018 and 21 October 2019. The tweets were collected in March 2022. Due to the platform's privacy policy, if a politician had deleted their account before this their timeline was no longer available. There were 628 active accounts. Table S1 gives an overview of each party, the initial number of handles and the percent of data loss. There was a wide range of data loss, with the lowest for the Green Party and the highest for the PPC. We used a custom version of the RTwitterV2 package (Kubli, 2021) to collect tweets and remove replies and retweets. After this, the dataset contained 128,694 tweets (3,784,954 words). We then refined using keywords that occur in political debates in Global North nations when discussing Muslims and/or Islam (e.g., Törnberg & Törnberg, 2016): 'Hijab', 'Koran', 'Mosque', 'Halal', 'Sharia', 'Muslim', 'Islam', 'Veil', 'Headscarf', 'Niqab' and 'Burka'. These were lemmatized and used as 'or' terms.

The refined dataset contained 808 tweets (27,320 words) from 204 politicians, meaning about a third wrote tweets containing at least one keyword. To gauge how much Muslims/Islam were discussed in comparison with a commonly related topic in Global North nations, we ran a search using two lemmatized keywords connected to immigration: 'immigra' and 'migra'. This resulted in 1096 tweets, meaning that although tweets relating to Muslims and/or Islam were a small proportion of the initial total (0.68%) they were only slightly less common than tweets relating to immigration (0.85%). Table S2 shows the breakdowns of total tweets/party. The final column is a ratio of tweets per politician to give a sense of how much each party tweeted about Muslims/Islam. The PPC tweeted the most (7.25 tweets/ politician) and the Greens the least (1.58 tweets/politician).

sections.paper_id: Shayegh et al. - 2024 - Canadian politicians' rhetoric on TwitterX Analysing prejudice and inclusion towards Muslims using
paragraphs: The first phase involved running the STM to address RQ1 and identify topics politicians discussed in relation to Muslim identities. The R code for the data collection and model are in the accompanying information (Data S1). STM is conducted based on co-occurrence probabilities, including offering a data-driven approach for the number of text examples (Roberts et al., 2019), meaning it is conducted relatively free from researchers' pre-existing conditions. However, it still requires pre-specification of the total number of topics to model. This can be obtained through a computational estimation or by manually considering the balance between cohesiveness and topic uniqueness of different models (Brookes & McEnery, 2019;Kosinski et al., 2016). For our data, the computational estimation was 68. We judged this topic number would not produce a cohesive analysis and, therefore, opted for manual comparison between multiple models.

We set a range of k topics between 5 and 15. This was an arbitrary choice but was viewed as a starting point. The STM output consisted of a word stem list and the top 20 example tweets for every k in our pre-specified range. All authors independently assessed the internal similarity and external differences in the example tweets in each topic for each model. Preliminary topic names were given, and difficult or incoherent topics were flagged. We relied primarily on the text examples to make decisions because, like others, we found the word stems difficult to interpret and classify (Brookes & McEnery, 2019). We then collectively discussed the results. We first identified the initial range as adequate as there was consensus that above 12 or 13 the models contained topics that were thematically mixed and/or incoherent. We then narrowed our k range from 8 to 11 and examined these models in greater depth. This iterative process continued until consensus was achieved on topic names and number. This first phase was conducted from a positivist position as language was taken at face value and analysed at the level of explicit meaning (Willig, 2017).

sections.paper_id: Shayegh et al. - 2024 - Canadian politicians' rhetoric on TwitterX Analysing prejudice and inclusion towards Muslims using
paragraphs: We chose 10 topics as optimal because this gave a good range of distinction, while not producing any incoherency. Table S3 shows the topics, word lists and occurrence percentages. The latter are provided to give the reader a sense of relative spread of topics across the data. Figures 1-10 show the probabilities for each topic to be tweeted across the chosen period. The first seven topics are ordered by the different ways inclusion was cultivated: through group actions and values (topics 1-4) and solidarity against hate (topics 5-7). We then placed topics 8-10 last as they were highly polarized between the parties. Below, we discuss each topic in detail and follow our two-step process by presenting what the politicians talked about before moving to how and why they did so. Example tweets (with location information removed) are presented to support our claims. Some topics were closely related, and for brevity, we discuss these together.

paragraphs: Politicians commonly tweeted about Muslim community initiatives. The STM distinguished three types of activities as indicated by the topic names. There was significant higher order overlap across the example texts and the model categorizations appeared to be due to relative emphasis rather than clear-cut

Additionally, politicians linked the positive actions at an ideological level to values of kindness and generosity. This rhetorical work of emphasizing charitable contributions to society, and linking these to universal human values, seemed to aim at cultivating a shared identity. One of the ways that perceptions of ingroup prototypicality can be increased is to be seen as working for the group (Steffens et al., 2014). Here, it seems the politicians sought to depict Canadian Muslims as working towards the common good of society to expand the national ingroup boundaries to include them. Topic 2, 'Community Events' was similar but focused more on hosting recreational events than volunteering work. This example similarly highlights a community initiative, but with additional emphasis on the politician's active support. Those seeking leadership roles can increase perceptions of suitability by demonstrating they can work to achieve group goals (Steffens et al., 2014). Therefore, politicians presented themselves as leaders who worked for the interests of minority communities and for anyone in Canadian society who valued an inclusive national identity. Lastly, what was interesting about these three topics is that most parties contributed tweets towards it, including the Conservative party, despite our expectations for right-leaning parties to produce more prejudicial rhetoric. The one exception was the nativist populist PPC, who had a near-zero probability of contributing tweets.

paragraphs: Topic 4 involved discussing religious festivals connected to Islam (Ramadan, Eid al-Fitr, Eid al-Adha) in two ways. First was to acknowledge an important religious event for Muslim communities and extend good wishes. This appeared to be a mild form of legitimization for the presence of Islamic festivals within Canadian society. Second was to connect broader, common values:

For Muslims, tonight marks the beginning of Ramadan, a month of fasting during the day from sunrise until sunset. It is a time of spiritual reflection, and a chance to reaffirm values of generosity, compassion and gratitude. Ramadan Mubarak.

There are similarities here to the 'Community Achievements' topic in emphasizing shared values. However, here the depictions of commonality are conducted through community-specific religious practices. Connecting these specifics to more general values appeared to aim towards legitimizing the presence of Islamic festivals in a non-Muslim majority nation. This is an important distinction

3 Community outreach. 0.00 0.25 0.50 0.75 Jan 2019 Apr 2019 Jul 2019 Oct 2019 Date Probability 20448309, 2024, 2, Downloaded from https://bpspsychub.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/bjso.12707 by University Of Chicago Library, Wiley Online Library on [05/02/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

because anti-Muslim rhetoric often invokes the notion of cultural value change due to continuing practices connected to religious backgrounds (Verkuyten, 2013). In such narratives, religious differences are essentialized as inherently incompatible and, therefore, constructed as representing a cultural threat. Conversely, within this topic, the politicians invoked universally shared values of generosity and kindness as underpinning group-specific religious practices. That is, the intergroup differences are implicitly positioned as superficial compared with the deeper ideological values shared between members of different groups. Like topics 1-3, the PPC was alone in having a near zero probability of contribute tweets.

paragraphs: Topics 5-7 encompassed different national and international events but shared an overarching focus on the connection between hate speech and violent attacks against Muslim communities. The topic 'Quebec Mosque Attack Remembrance' concerned the anniversary for victims of the 2017 terrorist attack on the Islamic Cultural Centre in Quebec City. There were two different forms this could take; first, the remembrance was linked to religious discrimination in general:
Yesterday, on the anniversary of the 2017 Quebec Mosque shooting, MPs of all parties lit candles in their windows in solidarity with all victims of anti-religious violence, regardless of faith.

Although this tweet invokes solidarity with victims of religious persecution, it does not acknowledge the specific subgroup persecution. Therefore, the superordinate group identification with suffering 'regardless

of faith' appears to downplay the group-specific persecution of Muslim Canadians. This can be contrasted with the second form of tweets in this topic:

We gathered by the Centennial Flame on […] tonight to remember those who lost their lives while praying at a mosque in Quebec 2 years ago. We reaffirmed our commitment to stand up against islamophobia and all forms of hate, ignorance, stereotyping and discrimination!

This tweet also links the tragedy to general concerns of prejudice, but it includes subgroup specifics of Islamophobia and the community-specific activity that led to the victims being targeted ('while praying at a mosque'). A similar pattern could be observed in topic 6 'Christchurch Mosque attack', which contained tweets relating to the terrorist attacks on two mosques in New Zealand that occurred in March 2019; politicians commonly responded with condemnation for the attacks, but whereas some acknowledged the subgroup specifics of this event:

Horrified by the mass shootings in Christchurch. My heart breaks for the worshippers at these two mosques. Canada stands with its New Zealand friends in condemning these violent acts of terror and Islamophobia.
Others did not:

My heart goes out to the victims of the monstrous shooting at two mosques in Christchurch, New Zealand. Therefore, although all tweets within these topics sought to depict solidarity with victims, only some acknowledged subgroup-specific prejudice. The PPC was alone in having a near zero probability for both topics. For the others, there did not appear to be any party-level patterns to whether subgroup specifics were acknowledged or not. Topic 7, 'Hate Speech and Hate Crimes', also contained tweets expressing concern with the violent consequences of Islamophobia, but with a more general focus on how prejudice and violence towards Muslims were linked within Canada, such as statistical trends in hate crimes. Further, some politicians discussed how Muslim communities themselves were responding:

Tonight I joined […], and the vibrant Muslim community at […]. A recent target of hate crime, they are doing great work to make […] a better place. Canada is made stronger by its diversity, and we will not tolerate hate crimes or hate speech of any kind.

Tweets of this nature moved beyond presenting Muslim communities as victims to show how they were working towards a better society despite these experiences. They therefore depicted them as resilient and highlighted their agency in combating hatred. Again, the PPC had a near-zero probability to contribute tweets.

paragraphs: This topic concerned the relationship between religion and liberal democracy and was highly polarized along party lines. The relationship was discussed through two related issues: Islamophobia and Sharia law. In relation to Islamophobia, the concept was constructed differently depending on the political party. Politicians from the Liberal, New Democratic, Conservative and Green parties expressed a concern for the impacts of Islamophobic rhetoric on society:

E 6 Christchurch Mosque attack. 0.00 0.25 0.50 0.75 1.00 Jan 2019 Apr 2019 Jul 2019 O ct 2019 Date Probability 20448309, 2024, 2, Downloaded from https://bpspsychub.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/bjso.12707 by University Of Chicago Library, Wiley Online Library on [05/02/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License This example depicts Islamophobia as a serious issue and implicates political leaders as part of the problem. Most of the parties advocated for addressing Islamophobia as a key part of protecting religious minorities within liberal democratic societies. However, for the PPC politicians the topic of Islamophobia was discussed differently:

Trudeau wants to give Muslims a nation-wide "safe-space" where they can exist free from any criticism. He champions M-103, which promises an "all of government approach" to tackling "Islamophobia", a term Trudeau's team refused to define.

paragraphs: This topic was primarily discussed by the PPC. It had two interrelated aspects which we tried to encompass in the name. These were Canadian values in relation to Muslim groups, and how this was treated by the media and/or government. The discussions of Canadian values were often implicit and only obliquely referred to the PPC's concerns: Discussions essential to protect our Canadian values. PPC Candidates discuss Globalism, Islamism, and Immigration. .00 0.25 0.50 0.75 Jan 2019 Apr 2019 Jul 2019 O ct 2019 Date Probability 20448309, 2024, 2, Downloaded from https://bpspsychub.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/bjso.12707 by University Of Chicago Library, Wiley Online Library on [05/02/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License | 871 PREJUDICE AND INCLUSION ON TWITTER/X

The ideological work is not as explicit as the previous rhetoric over Sharia Law, but there appeared to be similar implicit notions. First is the idea that Canadian values need protection, which is then connected to the notion of 'Islamism'. This implies Canadian values are under threat from Muslims, and further, that the PPC works to protect them. The other aspect of this topic involved depicting how these issues were treated by elite social actors:

The Islamic Party of […] is a Pro-Sharia law Muslim party that has a goal of making Islam the dominant legal system of Canada. But you won't hear about it from the CBC -the billion-dollar taxpayer-funded propaganda arm of Trudeau's Liberal Party!

The politician not only portrays Islam as a threat, but also that discussing this is systematically suppressed by media/political elites. The publicly funded Canadian Broadcast Company (CBC) is depicted as the 'propaganda arm' of the government, and furthermore, that it conceals 'the truth' about Muslims from the public. The tweets within this topic commonly depicted informational control by elites (e.g., 'leftist indoctrination machine') and referenced attacks on the PPC by mainstream political/media sources. They therefore construed a climate of targeted political disenfranchisement through constructions of both the party, and their supporters, being attacked when speaking 'the truth' and/or having access to 'true' information denied.

paragraphs: The final topic contained tweets about the relationships between people from different faith communities. It was another polarized topic between the PPC and the other parties. For the Liberals, Conservatives, New Democrats and Greens, interfaith relations were discussed through communal actions:

E 9 Disenfranchisement and Canadian values. 0.00 0.25 0.50 0.75 Jan 2019 Apr 2019 Jul 2019 O ct 2019 Date Probability 20448309, 2024, 2, Downloaded from https://bpspsychub.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/bjso.12707 by University Of Chicago Library, Wiley Online Library on [05/02/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

Delighted to be at the Mosaic Interfaith: From Abraham 3 Faiths! Great to be with the Muslim, Jewish, & Christian community members and scholars at the Jaffari Islamic Centre. Diversity is our strength! Tweets of this form commonly referenced successful interfaith initiatives and worked to emphasize that relations in Canadian society were positive and characterized by norms of peace and mutual support.

Conversely, for the PPC, interfaith relations were discussed differently: Does this include Imam's and their violent words of hatred for non-Islamic peoples like Christians & Jews? Trudeau warns of 'meaningful financial consequences' for social media giants that don't combat hate speech.

As in the example above, the PPC portrayed Muslims as prejudiced towards other faiths. They implied that interfaith relations were fractured and antagonistic and placed the blame for the antagonism onto Muslim groups. These constructions potentially aimed at cultivating intergroup anxiety and/or distrust towards Muslims. This appeared to be another way the PPC sought to delegitimize the presence of Muslims, by depicting them as a source of tension and division with other religious faiths in society.

sections.paper_id: Shayegh et al. - 2024 - Canadian politicians' rhetoric on TwitterX Analysing prejudice and inclusion towards Muslims using
Shayegh et al. - 2024 - Canadian politicians' rhetoric on TwitterX Analysing prejudice and inclusion towards Muslims using
paragraphs: We investigated Canadian politicians' rhetoric on Twitter in relation to Muslims/Islam in the year leading up to the October 2019 Canadian Federal Election. Through a STM analysis, we identified 10

E 1 0 Interfaith relations. 0.00 0.25 0.50 0.75 Jan 2019 Apr 2019 Jul 2019 Oct 2019 Date Probability 20448309, 2024, 2, Downloaded from https://bpspsychub.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/bjso.12707 by University Of Chicago Library, Wiley Online Library on [05/02/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License | 873 PREJUDICE AND INCLUSION ON TWITTER/X topics: Community Achievements, Community Events, Community Outreach, Religious Festivals, Quebec Mosque Attack Remembrance, Christchurch Mosque Attack, Hate Crimes and Hate Speech, Religion and Liberal Democracy, Disenfranchisement and Canadian Values, and Interfaith Relations.

Overall, our findings suggest that Canadian politicians discussed Muslims/Islam through more inclusive rhetoric than prejudicial. Four of the topics emphasized the positive actions and contributions of Canadian Muslims to society and three condemned the prejudice and violence Muslims faced. Finally, although the last three topics contained prejudicial rhetoric, this was only the case for the PPC, as the remaining parties discussed the topics from an inclusive perspective. This is interesting when compared to rhetorical climates in other Global North contexts, particularly Europe. The presence of nativist populist parties can encourage a rightward shift in right-leaning mainstream parties, as they seek to compete electorally on issues like immigration (Krzyżanowski, 2020;Rydgren, 2005). Over time this can lead to a greater normalization of the prejudicial othering of religious minorities (Wodak, 2020). However, our STM analysis suggests that, at least as far as Twitter posts about Muslims/Islam are concerned, the PPC has not yet caused similar shifts in Canada.

Despite a greater number of inclusive topics overall, some contained aspects that questioned the effectiveness of the inclusive conceptions of national identity. The most problematic of these were the topics discussing terrorist attacks on Muslims. Although most tweets sought to cultivate solidarity with victims by linking the attacks with wider societal efforts at combating religious hatred, some ignored Muslim-specific prejudice. This form of inclusive rhetoric appeared to share similarities with 'colour blind' approaches to racism (McNeill & Vollhardt, 2020). One could characterize the tweets invoking a need to combat prejudice 'regardless of faith' as a 'religion blind' approach because they implicitly neglected subgroup-specific Islamophobia. Such 'blind' approaches are ineffective as they can discourage minority group members from mobilizing to demand equal rights and make majority group members less likely to notice discrimination (Apfelbaum et al., 2010). The absence of subgroup acknowledgement could therefore serve to create an 'all lives matter' effect (McNeill & Vollhardt, 2020) and obscure the specific discrimination that Canadian Muslims face.

There were still several ways that politicians sought to rhetorically expand the national ingroup boundaries to include Muslims. In topics 1-3, charitable contributions and cross-community engagement were emphasized. This was significant given rhetorical tropes of outgroup insularity (e.g., 'ghettos'), which are a common way to delegitimize the presence of minority ethno-religious identities (e.g., Hogan & Haltinner, 2015). Additionally, the invocation of shared values can cultivate 'we-ness' (Reicher et al., 2006) and is something Muslim groups themselves have done in contexts where they are a religious minority (Cantori, 2022). Finally, subgroup-specific religious practices were acknowledged and celebrated by most parties, which included using these to cultivate shared values. Again, these represent important forms of inclusion in contrast to European contexts, where inclusion is often limited to cultural aspects, resulting in religious practices associated with Islam being viewed as problematic for the national ingroup (Bangstad, 2013;Rissanen, 2021).

However, our STM analysis suggests that, at least as far as Twitter posts about Muslims/Islam are concerned, the PPC has not yet caused similar shifts in Canada.

Despite a greater number of inclusive topics overall, some contained aspects that questioned the effectiveness of the inclusive conceptions of national identity. The most problematic of these were the topics discussing terrorist attacks on Muslims. Although most tweets sought to cultivate solidarity with victims by linking the attacks with wider societal efforts at combating religious hatred, some ignored Muslim-specific prejudice. This form of inclusive rhetoric appeared to share similarities with 'colour blind' approaches to racism (McNeill & Vollhardt, 2020). One could characterize the tweets invoking a need to combat prejudice 'regardless of faith' as a 'religion blind' approach because they implicitly neglected subgroup-specific Islamophobia. Such 'blind' approaches are ineffective as they can discourage minority group members from mobilizing to demand equal rights and make majority group members less likely to notice discrimination (Apfelbaum et al., 2010). The absence of subgroup acknowledgement could therefore serve to create an 'all lives matter' effect (McNeill & Vollhardt, 2020) and obscure the specific discrimination that Canadian Muslims face.

There were still several ways that politicians sought to rhetorically expand the national ingroup boundaries to include Muslims. In topics 1-3, charitable contributions and cross-community engagement were emphasized. This was significant given rhetorical tropes of outgroup insularity (e.g., 'ghettos'), which are a common way to delegitimize the presence of minority ethno-religious identities (e.g., Hogan & Haltinner, 2015). Additionally, the invocation of shared values can cultivate 'we-ness' (Reicher et al., 2006) and is something Muslim groups themselves have done in contexts where they are a religious minority (Cantori, 2022). Finally, subgroup-specific religious practices were acknowledged and celebrated by most parties, which included using these to cultivate shared values. Again, these represent important forms of inclusion in contrast to European contexts, where inclusion is often limited to cultural aspects, resulting in religious practices associated with Islam being viewed as problematic for the national ingroup (Bangstad, 2013;Rissanen, 2021).

sections.paper_id: Shayegh et al. - 2024 - Canadian politicians' rhetoric on TwitterX Analysing prejudice and inclusion towards Muslims using
paragraphs: The PPC used common anti-Muslim narratives found in the United States and Europe, providing further support for the concept of nativist populist parties adapting and learning from one another (Rydgren, 2005). First, they constructed Muslims as a threat to Canadian identity based on depictions of outgroup change, in which speakers construe minority identities as outgroups who seek to alter the nation away from familiar ingroup values (Hogan & Haltinner, 2015;Verkuyten, 2013) and who therefore pose an existential threat to the national ingroup identity (Portice & Reicher, 2018). Second, the PPC attempted to legitimize their prejudice through claims that they worked in the public interest in an informational climate beset by elite control. Nativist populists commonly use elite outgroups to construe their prejudicial rhetoric as representative of the public (Sakki & Pettersson, 2016;Wood & Finlay, 2008) and as part of their wider efforts to control the ingroup informational influence (Shayegh et al., 2022). Thus, to further justify their prejudicial agenda, the PPC depicted collusion between elites to position themselves as legitimate sources of information truly working for the Canadian public.

The PPC also employed a distinctive strategy to foster anti-Muslim prejudice: redefining the concept of Islamophobia. They sought to delegitimize the presence of Canadian Muslims by depicting legislation designed to protect them from discrimination as an imposition of an ill-defined concept granting Muslims unique social advantages. These two aspects aimed to conform to social norms of 'rationality' and 'fairness'. This appeared to be a contextual variation of the 'prejudice problematic', in which the concept of prejudice itself is a socially defined norm, and therefore open to rhetorical influence (Dixon & Levine, 2012). Those who seek to cultivate prejudice against specific social groups often endeavour to align their claims with notions of rationality. This is rooted in the common belief that true prejudice is irrational, and therefore, if one's views can be portrayed as rational deliberation, they cannot be prejudiced (Billig et al., 1988).

Although prejudicial rhetoric appeared to be confined to a single party, and one that has failed to gain any seats in national elections, the PPC's rhetorical influence can still be significant. Another unsuccessful nativist populist party in national elections was the United Kingdom Independence Party (UKIP). The lack of seats reflected difficulties smaller parties have in a 'first past the post' voting system (the same used in Canada), rather than a lack of influence on public debate (Goodwin, 2011). The influence of the PPC could be understood similarly, not by their share of electoral seats but by how they influence the discourses surrounding specific topics. Examining the PPC's rhetoric therefore allows us to delve deeper into the implications of their narratives and the potential ways they shape the conversation about Canadian Muslims.

sections.paper_id: Shayegh et al. - 2024 - Canadian politicians' rhetoric on TwitterX Analysing prejudice and inclusion towards Muslims using
paragraphs: Although our study sought to gain a representative view of rhetoric across the political spectrum, data loss due to account deletion reduced representativeness, especially for the PPC. This could have altered the findings, as those members of the party who deleted their profiles may have produced some inclusive rhetoric, meaning the party's relative lack was not as extreme as it appeared in our data set. Additionally, it is important within methodological pluralist approaches to recognize the contingency of any data/model upon which interpretation is based (Törnberg & Uitermark, 2021). In this study, the second phase of analysis was conducted on a text model built primarily by the STM. This structured how we encountered the data for the second phase and, therefore, influenced how we interpreted meaning versus solely conducting a rhetorical analysis on the initial tweet data.

Finally, in considering future rhetoric-based social media research, this will likely become more difficult as platforms continue to restrict API access. Academic research using digital data has at times prioritized expediency over societal value and given only cursory consideration to issues like consent for collection and deletion rights (Tromble, 2021). Although these are important issues to address, there is an argument to be made for distinguishing public figures/institutions as special cases as justification for collection without consent (Keuchenius et al., 2021). As social media platforms become increasingly essential political campaign tools, posts can be seen as akin to campaign speeches and news interviews, as each is produced explicitly to influence public understandings. To ensure greater data security, efforts are being made to collaborate with social media companies to continue to allow researchers access (Tromble, 2021). However, it remains to be seen whether the same level of collection can be achieved (e.g., all political candidates running in an election) or whether companies will impose restricted criteria (e.g., only general party accounts). It is also unclear whether access to such collaborations will be available to all researchers, or favour institutions with larger financial resources and/or from Global North nations. Ultimately, we believe that restrictions to APIs will hamper efforts to analyse politicians' rhetoric and, therefore, impact how societies understand their own narratives and hold people in positions of power to account.
